whatToFeed: Nitrogen-rich liquid organic fertilizer or blood meal to encourage rapid leaf growth.
pruningTips: Harvest outer leaves continuously at the base to allow inner leaves to grow, rather than pruning the top.
flavorProfile: Earthy, mineral-rich leaves similar to spinach, with a mild, slightly sweet, and crunchy stalk.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow seeds outdoors in mid-to-late April as soon as soil can be worked; sow again in August for fall.
whenToHarvest: Harvest individual outer leaves when they reach 6-10 inches tall for optimal flavor and texture.
usesForLargeHarvests: Sautéing with garlic, steaming stems separately, adding to soups, freezing after blanching.
hardinessNote: Biennial grown as an annual. Tolerates moderate winter frosts well into December in Zone 6b.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly into the garden 1/2 inch deep, spaced 2-4 inches apart in rows. Once seedlings emerge, thin them to a final spacing of 6-12 inches apart. Water gently but thoroughly immediately after sowing.
growthRequirements: Thrives in loose, deep, well-draining soil rich in organic matter with a preferred pH of 6.0 to 6.8. Requires uniform soil moisture to keep leaves crisp and prevent bolting. Tolerant of both chilly spring weather and summer heat.

whatToFeed: Balanced organic vegetable fertilizer high in potassium and phosphorus once flowering begins.
pruningTips: Pinch the terminal growing tips early on to induce branching; stake the plant to support heavy purple fruits.
flavorProfile: Mild, slightly smoky, and spongy texture that absorbs rich fats, oils, and savory seasonings beautifully.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start indoors early March; transplant outside late May once nighttime temperatures stay consistently above 50°F.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when skin is high-gloss, sleek, and slightly soft to the touch; dull skin means it is overripe and bitter.
usesForLargeHarvests: Baba ganoush, ratatouille, grilling, freezing roasted slices, pickling in olive oil.
hardinessNote: Grows strictly as a heat-loving annual in Zone 6/7.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds indoors 1/4 inch deep in seed-starting mix 8-10 weeks before the last frost. Use a heating mat to keep soil at 80-85°F for optimal germination. Transplant outdoors 18-24 inches apart only when soil is thoroughly warmed.
growthRequirements: Demands highly fertile, warm, and exceptionally well-draining sandy loam with a pH between 5.5 and 6.8. Requires heavy, deep watering at the root level and absolute maximum daily sun exposure to set and ripen fruit.

whatToFeed: Low-nitrogen organic fertilizer or light compost top-dress; avoids excessive vine growth at the expense of beans.
pruningTips: Pinch off the very tops of the vines once they reach the limit of your trellis system to force energy down into bean production.
flavorProfile: Sweet, crisp, and intensely green flavor with strings that develop only as they mature past peak harvest.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors mid-to-late May once all frost threat is gone and soil is warm.
whenToHarvest: Pick daily once pods are pencil-thin, smooth, and firm before the bean seeds bulge out visibly.
usesForLargeHarvests: Blanching and freezing, home canning, pickling (dilly beans), southern-style slow braising.
hardinessNote: Grows as an annual; must have a sturdy trellis, teepee, or netting framework.
plantingInstructions: Directly sow seeds 1 inch deep, spaced 3 inches apart at the base of a sturdy support system like a trellis or teepee. Do not soak seeds before planting in heavy soils to prevent rot. Thinning is rarely necessary.
growthRequirements: Prefers moderately fertile, loose, well-draining soil with a neutral pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Requires consistent, even moisture during flowering and pod development. Demands full sun and adequate air circulation through the trellis structure.

whatToFeed: High-nitrogen liquid organic fertilizer or fish emulsion to push lush, rapid green top growth.
pruningTips: Trim the top green tips occasionally if they become floppy, or trim the root tips slightly if planting from sets.
flavorProfile: Crisp and sharp with a very mild, sweet onion bite concentrated in the white lower stalks.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow seeds or plant sets in late March to mid-April; can repeat every 3 weeks for consecutive crops.
whenToHarvest: Pull the entire plant when stalks reach pencil thickness, or snip green tops to use like chives.
usesForLargeHarvests: Dehydrating for homemade onion powder, making scallion pancakes, stirring into stir-fries, freezing in oil.
hardinessNote: Perennial variety often grown as an annual, but can successfully overwinter in Zone 6b with heavy straw mulch.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in rows spaced 12 inches apart, or plant small bulb sets 1 inch deep spaced 2 inches apart. If growing from seed, thin the emerged seedlings to a final spacing of 2-3 inches.
growthRequirements: Requires rich, crumbly loam soil with excellent drainage and a near-neutral pH of 6.2 to 7.0. Shallow root systems need consistent, shallow watering to prevent drying out, along with careful weeding to avoid root disturbance.

whatToFeed: Balanced organic vegetable fertilizer in spring, switching to a high-phosphorus mix as bulbs begin to swell.
pruningTips: Never prune the leaves as they directly feed the underground bulb layers. Cut away any flower stalks immediately.
flavorProfile: Pungent, sharp, and spicy when raw, turning beautifully sweet with a deep crimson color when cooked.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant sets or transplants outdoors in early-to-mid April while soil is still cool.
whenToHarvest: Harvest in late summer when the top green leaves turn yellow and naturally fall flat onto the ground.
usesForLargeHarvests: Quick-pickling in vinegar, grilling on skewers, slicing fresh into burger toppings, raw salad mix-ins.
hardinessNote: Grows as a cool-season annual. Requires loose, non-compacted soil to build symmetric round bulbs.
plantingInstructions: Press onion sets root-down into loose soil about 1 inch deep, spacing them 4-5 inches apart in rows that are 12-18 inches apart. Ensure the pointed tip of the set is just barely poking through or level with the soil surface.
growthRequirements: Demands exceptionally loose, stone-free, sandy loam soil with a pH of 6.0 to 6.8. Needs steady water throughout the leaf-growing stage, but requires completely dry conditions once the bulb begins curing late in the season.

whatToFeed: Ample organic compost and balanced liquid feed. Keep sulfur levels low in soil to maximize natural sweetness.
pruningTips: Keep the growing area completely clear of weeds to eliminate root competition for the developing sweet bulbs.
flavorProfile: Exceptionally mild, juicy, and sweet with very low astringency or typical hot onion burn.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant sweet onion transplants outdoors in mid-april. Needs an early start to bulb before summer heat.
whenToHarvest: Dig up when the leaf necks weaken and collapse. Sweet onions have higher water content and thinner papers.
usesForLargeHarvests: French onion soup, fried onion rings, caramelizing in bulk, slicing fresh for sandwiches.
hardinessNote: Grows as an annual. Sweet varieties do not store long (use within 1-2 months of harvest).
plantingInstructions: Plant young, bare-root transplants 1 inch deep into well-amended soil. Space each plant 5-6 inches apart to allow room for wide bulb expansion, keeping rows roughly 12-15 inches apart. Water immediately to settle roots.
growthRequirements: Requires rich, deeply cultivated, highly porous soil with a pH of 6.2 to 6.8. Low-sulfur soil profiles are critical to cultivating a sweet flavor profile. Requires frequent watering due to higher tissue water concentrations.

whatToFeed: Nitrogen-rich fertilizer in early stages, moving to bone meal or high-potassium food by mid-summer.
pruningTips: Stop watering entirely 1-2 weeks before harvest once the tops begin to flag and turn brown to prevent root rot.
flavorProfile: Strong, classic, complex onion pungency when raw that mellows into rich, rich sweetness when caramelized.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant sets 1 inch deep in early April. Highly tolerant of early spring chilly weather.
whenToHarvest: Harvest in August when tops collapse. Leave bulbs in a dry, shaded, airy space for 2-3 weeks to fully cure.
usesForLargeHarvests: Long-term cellar storage, baseline aromatic for winter stews, dicing and freezing in bulk.
hardinessNote: Grows as an annual. Excellent thick, papery outer skins make this the ultimate storage onion variety.
plantingInstructions: Insert sets into rows 1 inch deep, ensuring a uniform 4-inch gap between each bulb to foster medium-to-large sizing. Space rows 12 inches apart. Firm the surrounding soil gently around the sets after planting.
growthRequirements: Thrives in well-drained, fertile loam heavily amended with aged compost, maintaining a pH of 6.0 to 6.8. Requires full sun and steady, moderate hydration. Soil compaction must be strictly avoided to prevent misshapen bulbs.

whatToFeed: Acidic organic fertilizer or well-rotted manure worked deep into trenches; avoid lime which encourages scab disease.
pruningTips: Continuously 'hill' soil or straw up around the green stalks as they grow tall to force more potatoes to set along the buried stem.
flavorProfile: Rich, intensely buttery flavor with a naturally yellow, moist, and versatile medium-starch flesh.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant chitted seed pieces 4 inches deep in mid-April when the first regional dandelions bloom.
whenToHarvest: Harvest 'new potatoes' 2-3 weeks after flowering ends, or wait until vines completely die back for full-sized storage tubers.
usesForLargeHarvests: Mashing with butter, roasting whole, potato salads, boiling for chowders.
hardinessNote: Cool-season annual. Tubers must be kept completely dark under soil to prevent them from turning green and toxic.
plantingInstructions: Cut seed potatoes into chunks containing at least 2 distinct 'eyes' and let the cuts dry out for 24 hours. Dig a trench 6 inches deep, drop pieces in face up spaced 12 inches apart, and backfill with 3-4 inches of loose soil.
growthRequirements: Requires loose, deeply tilled, acidic sandy loam soil with a preferred pH range of 4.8 to 5.5 to mitigate scab risk. Needs thorough drainage and consistent moisture, particularly during the mid-summer blossoming phase.

whatToFeed: Balanced organic vegetable fertilizer or bone meal incorporated during early planting and hilling phases.
pruningTips: Ensure consistent moisture during the blooming stage; dry soil blocks followed by water cause hollow heart cavities inside.
flavorProfile: Mild and earthy with a classic high-starch, dry, and light, fluffy texture when baked.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant seed potatoes in mid-to-late April. Requires a longer season to build giant baker size.
whenToHarvest: Harvest late September after foliage dies back entirely. Let skins cure in a cool, dark room for 2 weeks before cellaring.
usesForLargeHarvests: Homemade french fries, baking, thick potato leek soup bases, hashbrown shredding.
hardinessNote: Annual. Thick, rough, netting skin makes this type incredibly durable for deep winter storage.
plantingInstructions: Plant dried seed potato pieces 4 inches deep inside a trench, maintaining a 12-to-15-inch gap between pieces to give this large variety sufficient room to expand. Space multiple trenches 3 feet apart. Cover lightly at first.
growthRequirements: Requires deep, uncompacted, well-aerated sandy soil with a pH of 5.0 to 5.5. Demands highly uniform, regular irrigation schedules; erratic water triggers bumpy, misshapen tubers. Full sun is required all day long.

whatToFeed: Heavy feeder; apply high-nitrogen organic fish emulsion every two weeks until the main central head develops.
pruningTips: Cut the central main head at an angle to allow rain to run off; this triggers smaller side shoots to sprout continuously.
flavorProfile: Fresh, green, slightly sweet, and sulfurous notes that intensify if the vegetable is overcooked.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant early transplants out in mid-April for spring crop, or transplant in early August for a fall harvest.
whenToHarvest: Harvest immediately when the main head is tight and dense, right before individual green buds begin to split open into yellow flowers.
usesForLargeHarvests: Blanching and freezing florets, creamy broccoli soup, broccoli slaw from peeled woody stems.
hardinessNote: Cool-season annual. Frost actually improves flavor by triggering sugars to release inside the tissues.
plantingInstructions: Start seeds indoors 4-6 weeks ahead of transplanting, or purchase nursery starts. Set out seedlings 18 inches apart in rows spaced 24 inches apart. Bury stalks slightly deeper than original container levels to stabilize plants.
growthRequirements: Prefers heavy, cool, water-retentive clay-loam soils containing large amounts of compost, with a pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Demands absolute consistency in soil moisture and can tolerate partial shade, though full sun yields tighter heads.

whatToFeed: Needs constant, rich nutrition; compost tea supplemented with trace boron minerals to prevent hollow brown cores.
pruningTips: Perform 'blanching': when the white head is egg-sized, gather outer leaves up and tie them together to shield the curd from sun damage.
flavorProfile: Very mild, nutty, and slightly sweet with a dense, crisp crunch that easily mimics grains or starches.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Transplant outdoors in mid-to-late April. Best grown in fall (plant mid-July) as summer heat stunts curds.
whenToHarvest: Cut the head from the base stalk when it reaches 6-8 inches across but remains firm, tight, and completely smooth.
usesForLargeHarvests: Grating into cauliflower rice, freezing florets, pickling with hot peppers, roasting whole.
hardinessNote: Fickle cool-season annual; sensitive to temperature spikes which cause heads to split into loose 'button' clusters.
plantingInstructions: Set out robust 4-to-5-week-old transplants 18-24 inches apart in rows 30 inches apart. Press soil down firmly to anchor roots. Water deeply with an organic starter solution immediately after transplanting.
growthRequirements: Demands highly uniform growing conditions. Thrives in fertile, high-humus loam with a pH of 6.5 to 7.0. Soil must never dry out completely, as any moisture stress triggers premature, tiny button heads. Full sun is essential.

whatToFeed: Continuous light applications of organic fish emulsion and kelp meal mixtures.
pruningTips: Cut the very first central head quickly when it forms; this shocks the plant into generating tons of long, tender side shoots.
flavorProfile: Sweeter and more delicate than standard broccoli, with subtle, delicious hints of sweet asparagus.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Set out spring transplants in mid-April. Excellent choice for fall gardens if planted in early August.
whenToHarvest: Cut long, elegant stems with small leaf clusters when heads are small and loose, before any tiny blossoms pop open.
usesForLargeHarvests: Blistering in cast iron, charring on the grill, tossing into pasta stir-fries, steaming for side dishes.
hardinessNote: Annual hybrid of broccoli and Gai Lan (Chinese broccoli). Far more heat-tolerant and prolonged in production than standard broccoli.
plantingInstructions: Space transplants 12-15 inches apart within rows set 2 feet apart. Plant in early spring or mid-summer. Mulch heavily around the bases with straw or leaf litter immediately after planting to lock in soil coolness.
growthRequirements: Requires fertile, well-drained soil rich in organic matter with a stable pH range of 6.0 to 7.0. Needs constant moisture levels to support rapid stalk production, along with full sun to ensure optimal shoot tenderness.

whatToFeed: Feed the underlying garlic bed heavily with blood meal or fish emulsion in early spring. Stop feeding once scapes begin emerging.
pruningTips: Snap or cut the scape cleanly at its base where it emerges from the central leaves once it loops around into a full curl.
flavorProfile: Crisp, green, and snappy texture with a mild, sweet, and distinctly mellow garlic background flavor.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Grown implicitly from hardneck garlic cloves planted in mid-to-late October before ground freeze.
whenToHarvest: Harvest in June when the leafless flower stalk grows up and loops into a classic pig-tail curl.
usesForLargeHarvests: Garlic scape pesto, chopping into stir-fries, pickling in jars, mincing into marinades and compound herb butters.
hardinessNote: Product of hardneck garlic. Removing the scape is mandatory to force the plant to direct its energy into growing large underground bulbs.
plantingInstructions: Not planted independently. Cultivated strictly by planting individual cloves of hardneck garlic varieties 2 inches deep and 6 inches apart in the autumn, allowing the scapes to arise naturally from the centers in early summer.
growthRequirements: Dependent entirely upon the parent garlic plant. Requires rich, loose, perfectly drained soil with a pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Needs a cold winter chill period followed by full spring sun and moist, weed-free garden beds.

whatToFeed: Incorporate rich organic compost at planting. Provide a high-nitrogen top dress in March, then stop feeding by May.
pruningTips: Cut off all curly scapes in June to maximize bulb sizing. Reduce watering completely 2 weeks before lifting the crops.
flavorProfile: Sharp, pungent, hot, and sulfurous when raw, baking down into a rich, sweet, savory umami depth.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant separate individual cloves root-down 2 inches deep in mid-to-late October.
whenToHarvest: Dig up in July when the bottom 3-4 leaves turn completely brown but the top 4 leaves remain green.
usesForLargeHarvests: Braiding and hanging to cure, roasting whole heads, mincing into pasta sauces, making garlic oil.
hardinessNote: Overwinters effortlessly as a perennial, but treated as an annual crop. Requires a cold winter dormancy period to divide into cloves.
plantingInstructions: Separate bulbs into individual cloves right before planting. Insert each clove pointed-end up, root-end down, 2 inches deep into soft soil, leaving 6 inches between cloves. Cover rows with 4-6 inches of straw mulch for winter insulation.
growthRequirements: Requires exceptionally loose, crumbly, deeply-dug loam containing high levels of organic matter with a pH of 6.2 to 7.0. Demands crisp full sun. Proper drainage is absolutely paramount to keep the bulbs from rotting underground over winter.

whatToFeed: Top-dress with rich organic compost and a high-nitrogen organic fertilizer in early spring and right after the summer harvest ends.
pruningTips: Never cut back the feathery green ferns during summer; let them grow completely until they turn yellow/brown after winter's first hard frost, then cut to the ground.
flavorProfile: Earthy, green, and robust with a distinctly savory, nutty undertone.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant bare-root crowns outdoors in mid-to-late April as soon as the soil can be worked.
whenToHarvest: Harvest spears in spring by snapping them at ground level when they reach 6-8 inches tall and are pencil-thick; do not harvest until the plant's third year.
usesForLargeHarvests: Pickling spear jars, blanching and freezing, roasting with olive oil, asparagus soup bases.
hardinessNote: Long-lived perennial; crowns will produce spears annually for 15-20 years in Zone 6b.
plantingInstructions: Dig a trench 6-8 inches deep and 12 inches wide. Space crowns 12-18 inches apart inside the trench, spreading the roots out like a spider. Cover the crowns with 2 inches of soil, and continuously backfill the trench with dirt as the green shoots grow upward.
growthRequirements: Requires deep, loose, stone-free, and exceptionally well-draining sandy loam with a rich organic compost foundation and a neutral pH of 6.5 to 7.0. Demands absolute full sun and zero root competition from weeds.

whatToFeed: Incorporate heavy rotted manure or rich organic compost into the specialized soil mounds during late winter curation.
pruningTips: Carefully level the blanched soil mounds back down to flat ground in mid-summer after the cutting season concludes to let the ferns breathe.
flavorProfile: Delicate, sweet, and incredibly mild with a less bitter, less grassy note than green asparagus.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Sourced from established perennial crowns; build the blanching soil mounds over the rows in March.
whenToHarvest: Harvest in spring by carefully digging into the soil mounds to clip the spears at the base right before their tips pierce through into the sunlight.
usesForLargeHarvests: Traditional European poaching, pickling in white wine brine, pureeing into creamy white velvet soups.
hardinessNote: Perennial; requires intensive manual labor every spring to keep the stalks shielded from sunlight.
plantingInstructions: Grown from standard green asparagus crowns. In early spring of the third year, pile a 12-inch hill of loose soil, mulch, or dark plastic row covers completely over the crown row to block all ultraviolet light from reaching the emerging stalks.
growthRequirements: Requires loose, sandy soil that is highly light-porous and easy to hill up without packing into dense clods, keeping a pH of 6.5 to 7.0. Complete exclusion of light is required to prevent chlorophyll from developing.

whatToFeed: Heavy feeder; apply high-nitrogen fish emulsion and boron-trace fertilizers every two weeks during leaf scaling.
pruningTips: Snap off the lower leaf stems along the central stalk as the tiny sprouts develop underneath them; pinch the terminal top cluster 4 weeks before harvest to force all energy into tightening the remaining sprouts.
flavorProfile: Nutty, sweet, and intensely savory; bitterness drops away drastically if harvested after a deep frost.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start seeds indoors in mid-to-late May; transplant outdoors in early July for a reliable autumn and early-winter harvest.
whenToHarvest: Harvest from the bottom of the stalk upward when individual mini-cabbage heads feel hard, dense, and reach 1-1.5 inches in diameter.
usesForLargeHarvests: Roasting with balsamic vinegar, shredding fresh for slaws, freezing whole after a brief blanching, pickling.
hardinessNote: Long-season cool crop annual. Extremely frost-tolerant; plants easily survive temperatures down to 20°F in Western PA winter slots.
plantingInstructions: Start seeds indoors to avoid early summer field heat bugs. Set 4-to-6-week-old starts into the garden in July, spacing them 18-24 inches apart in rows spaced 30 inches apart. Bury the stem deep to anchor its top-heavy growth.
growthRequirements: Demands highly fertile, heavy, firm loam that holds moisture evenly and features a pH range of 6.5 to 7.5. Needs constant root dampness without pooling. Requires strong full sun and long, cool autumn temperatures to fully develop.
